attrs.alt: Admin Consent Flow for Multi-Tenant Apps

content.text: What is Admin Consent?

Admin consent is the mechanism by which an Azure AD / Entra ID Global Administrator (or Privileged Role Administrator) grants an application permission to access resources on behalf of the entire tenant. It is required when: (1) the application requests application permissions (no user context), (2) a delegated permission is marked admin-required, or (3) the tenant's consent policy blocks user consent.

The difference between user consent and admin consent: user consent applies only to the consenting user's tokens. Admin consent applies to all users in the tenant and is the only way to grant application permissions (used by daemon apps with no signed-in user).
Consent Types
Consent Type
Granted By
Scope
Required For
User consent
Individual user
Only that user's tokens
Low-risk delegated permissions (User.Read, openid, profile, offline_access)
Admin consent (delegated)
Tenant admin
All users in the tenant
High-risk delegated permissions (Mail.Read, User.ReadAll, Group.ReadWrite.All)
Admin consent (app-only)
Tenant admin
The application itself (no user)
All application permissions — daemon apps, background services
Admin Consent URL

The most common way to trigger admin consent for a multi-tenant app is to send the admin a crafted URL. The admin clicks it, reviews the permissions, and grants consent — creating the service principal in their tenant.
URL Format

# Admin consent URL — send this to the customer's Global Administrator
# Required parameters:
#   client_id    — your app's appId (Application ID)
#   redirect_uri — must be registered in your app; receives the post-consent callback
#   state        — optional CSRF protection value your app can verify

CLIENT_ID='<your-app-client-id>'
REDIRECT_URI='https%3A%2F%2Fapp.contoso.com%2Fadmin-consent-callback'
STATE='csrf-nonce-12345'

# For work/school accounts (AzureADMultipleOrgs):
ADMIN_CONSENT_URL="https://login.microsoftonline.com/common/adminconsent?client_id=${CLIENT_ID}&redirect_uri=${REDIRECT_URI}&state=${STATE}"

# Or target a specific tenant directly (preferred — confirms the customer's tenant):
TENANT='customer-tenant.onmicrosoft.com'
ADMIN_CONSENT_URL="https://login.microsoftonline.com/${TENANT}/adminconsent?client_id=${CLIENT_ID}&redirect_uri=${REDIRECT_URI}&state=${STATE}"

echo $ADMIN_CONSENT_URL
Handling the Post-Consent Callback

# FastAPI example — handle the redirect_uri callback after admin consent
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request, HTTPException
import secrets

app = FastAPI()

# In-memory CSRF store (use Redis or DB in production)
pending_states: dict = {}

@app.get('/initiate-consent')
async def initiate_consent(tenant_id: str):
    state = secrets.token_hex(16)
    pending_states[state] = tenant_id
    client_id   = '<your-app-client-id>'
    redirect_uri = 'https://app.contoso.com/admin-consent-callback'
    url = (
        f'https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tenant_id}/adminconsent'
        f'?client_id={client_id}'
        f'&redirect_uri={redirect_uri}'
        f'&state={state}'
    )
    return {'consent_url': url}

@app.get('/admin-consent-callback')
async def consent_callback(request: Request):
    params = dict(request.query_params)
    state  = params.get('state')
    error  = params.get('error')

    if state not in pending_states:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail='Invalid state — possible CSRF')

    tenant_id = pending_states.pop(state)

    if error:
        # Admin declined or an error occurred
        return {'status': 'declined', 'error': params.get('error_description')}

    # Consent granted — store the tenant as onboarded
    admin_upn = params.get('admin')  # the UPN of the admin who consented
    return {'status': 'granted', 'tenant_id': tenant_id, 'admin': admin_upn}
Granting Admin Consent Programmatically

For automated onboarding pipelines, you can grant admin consent using the Microsoft Graph API without requiring a browser interaction. This requires the calling service principal to have the Application.Read.All and DelegatedPermissionGrant.ReadWrite.All (or AppRoleAssignment.ReadWrite.All) permissions in the target tenant.
Granting Delegated Permission Consent via Graph

# Run in the CUSTOMER tenant as a Global Administrator
Connect-MgGraph -TenantId '<customer-tenant-id>' `
    -Scopes 'Application.Read.All','DelegatedPermissionGrant.ReadWrite.All'

$appId = '<your-app-client-id>'

# Ensure the service principal exists in this tenant (created on first consent/sign-in)
$sp = Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "appId eq '$appId'"
if (-not $sp) {
    $sp = New-MgServicePrincipal -AppId $appId
    Write-Host 'Service principal created.'
}

# Grant admin consent for User.Read delegated permission (all users)
# ResourceId = Microsoft Graph service principal Id in this tenant
$graphSp = Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "appId eq '00000003-0000-0000-c000-000000000000'"

New-MgOauth2PermissionGrant -BodyParameter @{
    ClientId    = $sp.Id
    ConsentType = 'AllPrincipals'   # admin consent = all users
    ResourceId  = $graphSp.Id
    Scope       = 'User.Read openid profile offline_access'
}
Write-Host 'Admin consent granted for delegated permissions.'
Granting Application Permission Consent via Graph

# Grant admin consent for an application permission (app-only, no user)
Connect-MgGraph -TenantId '<customer-tenant-id>' `
    -Scopes 'Application.Read.All','AppRoleAssignment.ReadWrite.All'

$appId   = '<your-app-client-id>'
$sp      = Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "appId eq '$appId'"
$graphSp = Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "appId eq '00000003-0000-0000-c000-000000000000'"

# Find the appRole Id for the application permission you want to grant
# Example: User.Read.All (application permission) on Microsoft Graph
$roleId = ($graphSp.AppRoles | Where-Object {
    $_.Value -eq 'User.Read.All' -and $_.AllowedMemberTypes -contains 'Application'
}).Id

New-MgServicePrincipalAppRoleAssignment `
    -ServicePrincipalId $sp.Id `
    -BodyParameter @{
        PrincipalId = $sp.Id
        ResourceId  = $graphSp.Id
        AppRoleId   = $roleId
    }
Write-Host 'Application permission User.Read.All granted.'
Admin Consent Workflow (User Request Queue)

When a tenant has disabled user consent, individual users can trigger an admin consent request that goes into a review queue. Admins receive an email and can approve or deny requests from the Microsoft Entra portal.
Users who hit a permission-required screen see 'Request admin approval' instead of a grant button.
The request includes the user's justification text and the permissions being requested.
Global Administrators and Privileged Role Administrators can review requests at Microsoft Entra ID → Enterprise Applications → Admin consent requests.
Approved requests trigger a consent grant for all users in the tenant (not just the requesting user).
Denied requests notify the requester and log the denial to the audit log.
Configuring the Admin Consent Workflow via PowerShell
Revoking Admin Consent

Admin consent can be revoked from the Enterprise Applications blade or via the Graph API. Revoking consent does not delete the service principal — it removes the permission grant, so the app can no longer obtain tokens for those permissions until consent is re-granted.

# Revoke a delegated permission grant in the customer tenant
Connect-MgGraph -TenantId '<customer-tenant-id>' `
    -Scopes 'DelegatedPermissionGrant.ReadWrite.All'

$appId = '<your-app-client-id>'
$sp    = Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "appId eq '$appId'"

# Find and remove the OAuth2 permission grant
$grants = Get-MgOauth2PermissionGrant -Filter "clientId eq '$($sp.Id)'"
foreach ($grant in $grants) {
    Remove-MgOauth2PermissionGrant -OAuth2PermissionGrantId $grant.Id
    Write-Host "Revoked grant: $($grant.Scope)"
}

# Revoke an application role assignment
$assignments = Get-MgServicePrincipalAppRoleAssignment -ServicePrincipalId $sp.Id
foreach ($assignment in $assignments) {
    Remove-MgServicePrincipalAppRoleAssignment `
        -ServicePrincipalId $sp.Id `
        -AppRoleAssignmentId $assignment.Id
    Write-Host "Revoked app role: $($assignment.AppRoleId)"
}
Write-Host 'All consent revoked.'
KQL: Auditing Consent Activity

// All admin consent grants and revocations — last 90 days
AuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(90d)
| where OperationName in (
    'Consent to application',
    'Add delegated permission grant',
    'Remove delegated permission grant',
    'Add app role assignment to service principal',
    'Remove app role assignment from service principal'
  )
| extend AppName    = tostring(TargetResources[0].displayName)
| extend Actor      = InitiatedBy.user.userPrincipalName
| extend ConsentType = tostring(TargetResources[0]
    .modifiedProperties | where Name == 'ConsentType' | project NewValue)
| project TimeGenerated, OperationName, AppName, Actor, ConsentType, Result
| order by TimeGenerated desc
Consent Policies and User Consent Settings

Each Entra ID tenant has an authorization policy that controls whether users can self-consent to applications and which apps qualify for user consent. These settings are found in Microsoft Entra ID → Enterprise Applications → Consent and permissions → User consent settings.
Configuring User Consent Policy via PowerShell
Custom Permission Grant Policies

Custom permission grant policies let you define precisely which applications users can consent to — for example, allowing self-consent only for apps from your own publisher or from specific verified publishers in your trusted vendor list.

Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'Policy.ReadWrite.PermissionGrant'

# Create a custom permission grant policy
# Only allows user consent for apps from your own publisher (your tenant's publisher)
$customPolicy = New-MgPolicyPermissionGrantPolicy -BodyParameter @{
    Id          = 'my-company-app-policy'
    DisplayName = 'Allow consent for internal publisher apps only'
    Description = 'Users can consent to apps published by our company'
}

# Add an include condition: apps from a specific certified publisher
New-MgPolicyPermissionGrantPolicyInclude `
    -PermissionGrantPolicyId $customPolicy.Id `
    -BodyParameter @{
        PermissionType         = 'delegated'
        # clientApplicationPublisherIds limits to specific MPN publisher IDs
        ClientApplicationPublisherIds = @('<your-mpn-publisher-id>')
        # permissionClassification: 'low' = low-risk delegated permissions only
        PermissionClassification = 'low'
    }

# Apply the custom policy to default user role
Update-MgPolicyAuthorizationPolicy -BodyParameter @{
    PermissionGrantPolicyIdsAssignedToDefaultUserRole = @(
        "ManagePermissionGrantsForSelf.$($customPolicy.Id)"
    )
}
Write-Host 'Custom permission grant policy applied.'
Incremental and Dynamic Consent

Rather than requesting all permissions upfront on first sign-in (which can alarm users and prompt admin review), best practice is to request only what your app immediately needs, then request additional scopes incrementally when features that require them are first used.
Incremental Consent Example (Python MSAL)

import msal

CLIENT_ID    = '<your-app-client-id>'
AUTHORITY    = 'https://login.microsoftonline.com/common'
REDIRECT_URI = 'https://app.contoso.com/auth/callback'

app = msal.PublicClientApplication(client_id=CLIENT_ID, authority=AUTHORITY)

# Step 1: Sign in with minimal scopes (identity only)
base_scopes = ['openid', 'profile', 'offline_access']
result = app.acquire_token_interactive(scopes=base_scopes, redirect_uri=REDIRECT_URI)

# ... user is now signed in; app works with basic identity

# Step 2: When the user accesses the 'Email' feature, request Mail.Read
# MSAL checks cache first — only prompts if the scope was not previously granted
mail_scopes = ['Mail.Read']
accounts = app.get_accounts()
result = app.acquire_token_silent(scopes=mail_scopes, account=accounts[0])

if result is None:
    # Not in cache — need interactive consent for Mail.Read
    result = app.acquire_token_interactive(
        scopes=mail_scopes,
        account=accounts[0],
        redirect_uri=REDIRECT_URI,
        # prompt='consent' forces the consent screen even if previously granted
    )

if 'access_token' in result:
    print('Mail.Read access token acquired')
Terraform: Granting Admin Consent

The azuread_service_principal_delegated_permission_grant and azuread_app_role_assignment resources automate consent granting in Terraform. This is useful for internal tools or dev/test environments where you want infrastructure-as-code to provision both the app and its consent.
Common Consent Errors
Error Code
Message
Cause
Fix
AADSTS65001
User or administrator has not consented to use the app
No service principal or consent grant in the target tenant
Send admin consent URL; or have a user sign in interactively to trigger user consent
AADSTS700016
Application not found in directory
Service principal doesn't exist in the target tenant
Admin must grant consent first to create the SP; or use /common + interactive sign-in
AADSTS90094
Admin consent required
Permission is admin-required and user tried to self-consent
Send /adminconsent URL to tenant admin
AADSTS65004
User declined to consent
User clicked Cancel on the consent screen
Provide better UX explanation of why permissions are needed; try again
AADSTS50020
User account from identity provider does not exist in tenant
B2B guest used wrong tenant context
Use tenant-specific authority URL, not /common
Summary
Admin consent is required when an app requests application permissions (no user), admin-required delegated permissions, or when the tenant's policy blocks user self-consent.
The admin consent URL pattern is /adminconsent?client_id=...&redirect_uri=...&state=... — send this to the customer's Global Administrator.
After admin consent, Entra ID creates a service principal in the customer tenant and records the permission grants on it.
Programmatic consent grant uses New-MgOauth2PermissionGrant (delegated) and New-MgServicePrincipalAppRoleAssignment (application permissions) in the target tenant.
The admin consent workflow feature lets individual users request admin approval instead of being blocked entirely — configurable via the Authorization Policy.
Consent can be revoked at any time via the Enterprise Apps portal or by deleting the OAuth2 permission grants and app role assignments on the service principal.
